gene_region.label: DRD4 transcribed interval
gene_region.definition: Canonical DRD4 gene interval used by NCBI Gene and ClinGen for the GRCh37/hg19 assembly.

promoter_review_region.label: Operational promoter review window
promoter_review_region.definition: A practical 1 kb upstream window used by this app to decide whether the commonly studied DRD4 promoter region was included in the search.

promoter_hotspot_region.label: Promoter polymorphism hotspot
promoter_hotspot_region.definition: Approximate -809 to -521 interval highlighted in classic DRD4 promoter studies, including the rs747302 and rs1800955 regulatory hotspot.

gene_context.gene_summary: DRD4 encodes the dopamine D4 receptor, a dopamine-responsive GPCR that inhibits adenylyl cyclase and has been studied in attention, novelty-seeking, addiction, schizophrenia-related phenotypes, and social-emotional processing. On the hg19/GRCh37 assembly used by this project, DRD4 spans chr11:637,269-640,706 and covers 3,438 bp of the transcribed gene interval.
gene_context.clinical_context: The curated DRD4 records in this database are primarily research and pharmacogenomic context markers rather than high-penetrance diagnostic variants. When explicit ClinVar-style pathogenicity is not available, the app reports that status directly instead of implying a clinical-grade interpretation.
variant_effect_overview: Most commonly discussed DRD4 sequence findings are regulatory promoter polymorphisms or repeat-length polymorphisms with modest, context-dependent effects rather than monogenic pathogenic variants.

Promoter-region SNPs such as rs1800955 and rs747302 are usually interpreted as transcriptional or haplotype markers, while the exon III VNTR is usually interpreted as a receptor-signaling and behavioral-trait modifier.

Observed PASS variants inside the DRD4 interval should therefore be read as locus context markers unless they directly match a curated research variant or have independent clinical evidence outside this bundled database.
condition_research_overview: Promoter and repeat polymorphisms in DRD4 have been repeatedly studied in schizophrenia, ADHD, novelty-seeking, addiction, social-affect processing, and other dopaminergic behavioral phenotypes.
The strongest evidence in this bundled database is candidate-gene or locus-context research, not a diagnostic variant panel for a single disorder.
When the current run does not directly hit a seeded DRD4 variant, the app still reports the broader conditions and research themes most often associated with the promoter and gene-body literature.
gene_context.methylation_interpretation: Published DRD4 methylation studies have linked higher methylation to addiction risk, ADHD-related attentional symptoms, and male schizophrenia cohorts, but the direction and magnitude of those effects depend on tissue source and the exact CpG region that was measured. The bundled EPIC subset in this project mostly covers DRD4 gene-body and CpG-island probes plus one 3'UTR probe, so its beta values should be treated as regulatory context rather than a stand-alone diagnostic biomarker or a direct readout of the promoter assays used in many case-control papers.
methylation_effects: Higher DRD4 methylation is most often interpreted as a regulatory-state signal that may alter local transcription factor access or reflect lower effective promoter activity, rather than as a direct diagnosis by itself.
Different DRD4 CpG windows can behave differently across tissues, so promoter-focused papers and gene-body EPIC probes should not be assumed to be interchangeable measurements.
When DRD4 methylation is discussed together with promoter variants such as rs3758653, the typical interpretation is an mQTL-style regulatory interaction between sequence background and CpG state.
methylation_condition_research: Addiction and substance-use research: DRD4 promoter hypermethylation has been linked to heroin addiction risk and to shorter time from first heroin exposure to addiction in work that also studied rs3758653.
ADHD and treatment-response research: DRD4 methylation has been associated with symptom dimensions and with methylphenidate-response modulation in the context of prenatal maternal stress.

Psychiatric cohort research: male-specific schizophrenia associations and other neuropsychiatric case-control findings have been reported, but these remain tissue- and cohort-dependent rather than diagnostic biomarkers.

ClinGen gene page: DRD4 locus metadata on GRCh37
PMC5776619: DRD4 promoter hypermethylation and addiction risk
PMC3929639: male-specific DRD4 methylation association in schizophrenia
PubMed 26897359: DRD4 methylation and ADHD symptom dimensions
PubMed 29905488: DRD4 methylation, prenatal stress, and methylphenidate response in ADHD

variant_records.clinical_interpretation: rs3758653 is best interpreted as an upstream regulatory DRD4 marker with methylation-QTL and behavioral-association evidence. Published studies link it to nearby DRD4 CpG methylation levels, executive-time differences, and time from first heroin exposure to addiction, but it is not treated as a monogenic pathogenic variant.
variant_records.clinical_significance: Research-level association; no pathogenic or likely pathogenic assertion is curated for this SNP in the local database.
functional_effects: Acts as an upstream regulatory marker that has been associated with average DRD4 methylation levels in locus-level mQTL analyses.
Can be interpreted as part of a promoter haplotype background rather than a coding change to the dopamine D4 receptor protein.
associated_conditions: Heroin addiction timing and addiction-risk research
Regulatory methylation-QTL studies of the DRD4 locus
research_context: The C/T state is usually discussed as a regulatory background variable rather than a direct diagnostic allele call.
When this site is present with altered DRD4 methylation, the dominant interpretation is a sequence-to-methylation interaction rather than a protein-coding mechanism.
variant_records.usual_variant_note: Frequently discussed as a promoter-adjacent regulatory SNP rather than a core coding change.
variant_records.methylation_interpretation: This locus is especially relevant when DRD4 methylation is also being reviewed: rs3758653 has repeatedly been linked to DRD4 CpG methylation differences. In this workbench, methylation should still be read as supportive regulatory context because the bundled probe subset does not directly reproduce every promoter CpG from the published mQTL studies.

evidence.label: PubMed 34864042: rs3758653 mQTL and heroin addiction timing
PMC3538530: rs3758653-associated DRD4 methylation patterns
PMC1705471 / PubMed 17089069: Japanese schizophrenia promoter-variant study
PMC4833125: promoter polymorphism reporter dataset including rs3758653

literature_findings.phenotype: Heroin addiction latency and DRD4 promoter methylation in Chinese heroin-use cohorts
literature_findings.finding: TT carriers took the longest mean time from first heroin exposure to addiction (2.91 months), TC carriers were intermediate (2.57 months), and CC carriers were shortest (1.80 months); rs3758653 was also linked to several SNP-CpG correlations that were unique to the heroin-addiction group.

literature_findings.phenotype: Average DRD4 promoter methylation in lymphoblastoid cell lines with replication in postmortem brain tissue
literature_findings.finding: Mean methylation increased across genotype groups from CC (0.49) to CT (0.57) to TT (0.65) in the discovery sample, and the direction of effect was nominally replicated in superior frontal gyrus brain tissue, supporting rs3758653 as a local methylation-QTL.

literature_findings.phenotype: Schizophrenia case-control frequencies in a Japanese cohort
literature_findings.finding: Genotype frequencies were similar between schizophrenia cases (0.66/0.31/0.03) and controls (0.62/0.34/0.05), so this cohort did not support a strong standalone schizophrenia association for rs3758653.

literature_findings.phenotype: Luciferase-based DRD4 promoter activity in a neural cell line
literature_findings.finding: Reporter assays that introduced the rs3758653-linked -906T/C state into one-repeat and two-repeat promoter haplotypes did not show reproducible promoter-activity differences, suggesting that any functional effect is subtle or depends on broader haplotype context.

variant_records.clinical_interpretation: rs747302 (-616C>G) is a commonly studied DRD4 promoter-region polymorphism with phenotype-association evidence rather than diagnostic pathogenicity. Large schizophrenia meta-analysis did not support a robust association for this site, and post-mortem brain tissue work did not show a statistically significant mRNA effect; separate neuroimaging work has reported genotype-related differences in pediatric primary nocturnal enuresis cohorts.
variant_records.clinical_significance: Research-level association; no pathogenic or likely pathogenic classification is seeded for this promoter SNP.
functional_effects: Used mainly as a promoter haplotype and transcriptional-context marker in DRD4 regulatory studies.
Functional assays and post-mortem expression work suggest that any direct effect is likely subtle and context dependent rather than a large expression switch.
associated_conditions: General promoter haplotype studies of dopaminergic behavior
research_context: This SNP is usually interpreted in combination with nearby promoter polymorphisms instead of as a stand-alone high-impact locus.
A null or weak expression result in brain tissue does not exclude smaller context-specific regulatory effects in other assay systems.
variant_records.usual_variant_note: One of the classic DRD4 promoter polymorphisms routinely reviewed in candidate-gene studies.
variant_records.methylation_interpretation: Regional methylation may still matter biologically around rs747302, but current evidence supports a regulatory-modifier role rather than a methylation-defined diagnostic effect. Use the paired methylation panel to contextualize local epigenetic state, not to over-call this promoter SNP.

evidence.label: PMC1705471 / PubMed 17089069: Japanese schizophrenia promoter-variant study
PubMed 20468066: post-mortem mRNA study including rs747302
PMC5757990 / PubMed 29379288: schizophrenia meta-analysis
PMC5430843 / PubMed 28450726: rs747302-linked neuroimaging differences
PMC4833125: promoter polymorphism reporter dataset including rs747302

literature_findings.phenotype: Schizophrenia case-control frequencies in a Japanese cohort
literature_findings.finding: Genotype frequencies were similar between schizophrenia cases (0.09/0.44/0.47) and controls (0.10/0.39/0.50), so this cohort did not support a strong standalone rs747302 effect.

literature_findings.genotypes: C/G promoter genotypes pooled across 1,735 cases and 1,724 controls
literature_findings.finding: The pooled analysis did not detect a significant association between the -616 C>G polymorphism and schizophrenia, arguing against a robust population-wide effect at this site.

literature_findings.phenotype: DRD4 mRNA abundance in 28 human postmortem brain samples
literature_findings.finding: DRD4 mRNA levels did not differ significantly by rs747302 genotype, so the study did not find evidence for a large constitutive brain-expression effect.

literature_findings.phenotype: Primary nocturnal enuresis, arousal from sleep, and resting-state neuroimaging in children
literature_findings.finding: C-allele carriers had higher arousal-from-sleep scores and, within the primary nocturnal enuresis group, lower thalamic gray-matter volume and lower thalamic functional connectivity density than GG children.

literature_findings.phenotype: Luciferase-based DRD4 promoter activity in a neural cell line
literature_findings.finding: Reporter assays that inserted the -616G/C state into multiple promoter haplotypes did not show reproducible activity differences, suggesting that any direct regulatory effect is modest or highly context dependent.

variant_records.clinical_interpretation: rs1800955 (-521C>T) is a common DRD4 promoter polymorphism with research-level evidence for altered transcriptional regulation and small effect-size psychiatric associations. Early reporter assays found lower transcriptional efficiency for the T allele, while later post-mortem brain tissue work did not find a statistically significant genotype-expression relationship; meta-analysis suggests the CC genotype may modestly increase schizophrenia risk, but this site is not treated as a monogenic pathogenic variant.
variant_records.clinical_significance: Research-level association; no pathogenic or likely pathogenic ClinVar-style assertion is seeded for this promoter SNP.
functional_effects: The T allele showed lower transcriptional efficiency in early reporter assays, consistent with a promoter-regulation effect rather than a protein-coding effect.
Later brain-expression studies reported mixed or null expression consequences, so the locus is best treated as a context-dependent regulatory marker.
associated_conditions: Promoter regulation studies of dopaminergic behavior
research_context: This is the best-known DRD4 promoter SNP and is often the first locus checked when the literature mentions DRD4 promoter regulation.
Different papers focus on different alleles and cohort definitions, so effect direction should be read in the context of the cited study.
variant_records.usual_variant_note: The best-known DRD4 promoter SNP in schizophrenia and emotion-processing candidate-gene studies.
variant_records.methylation_interpretation: Interpret rs1800955 alongside regional DRD4 methylation only as regulatory context. Differential methylation in the broader DRD4 locus has been reported in addiction, ADHD, and schizophrenia cohorts, but those effects are tissue-specific and do not convert rs1800955 into a diagnostic methylation biomarker.

evidence.label: PubMed 10329380: -521C>T transcriptional efficiency and schizophrenia association
PMC1705471 / PubMed 17089069: Japanese schizophrenia promoter-variant study
PubMed 20468066: post-mortem brain mRNA study of rs1800955 and rs747302
PMC5757990 / PubMed 29379288: DRD4 schizophrenia meta-analysis
PMC9321090 / PubMed 35574658: rs1800955 and emotion-processing phenotype

literature_findings.phenotype: DRD4 promoter transcription and schizophrenia allele frequency in Japanese adults
literature_findings.finding: Transient reporter assays showed that the T allele reduced transcriptional efficiency by about 40% relative to the C allele, while the C allele was more frequent in schizophrenia cases (0.48) than in controls (0.41), so the authors proposed a tentative risk signal that required replication.

literature_findings.phenotype: Schizophrenia case-control frequencies in a Japanese cohort
literature_findings.finding: Genotype frequencies were very similar in schizophrenia cases (0.34/0.48/0.19) and controls (0.34/0.50/0.16), so this cohort did not support a strong standalone rs1800955 effect.

Across 10 case-control datasets, the CC genotype showed a modest but statistically significant increase in schizophrenia risk (OR 1.218, 95% CI 1.050-1.413), while the locus still behaved like a small-effect susceptibility marker rather than a diagnostic variant.

literature_findings.phenotype: DRD4 mRNA abundance in 28 human postmortem brain samples
literature_findings.finding: DRD4 mRNA levels did not show a statistically significant relationship with rs1800955 genotype, so the in vivo brain-expression data did not reproduce a large constitutive expression effect.

literature_findings.phenotype: Positive affect during negative or neutral social stimuli in two nonclinical emotion-processing cohorts
literature_findings.finding: Across film-clip and Cyberball tasks, CC individuals reported higher positive affect than T-carrier individuals when the social context was neutral or negative, supporting a context-dependent effect on social-emotional processing.

variant_records.common_name: a 120-bp duplication in the 5' flanking region
variant_records.clinical_interpretation: A 120-bp duplication in the DRD4 5' flanking region has been reported as part of the classical promoter haplotype architecture and can alter transcription-factor binding context. It is biologically relevant in promoter studies, but the SNP-oriented VCF preview in this app does not directly genotype this duplication.
variant_records.clinical_significance: Research-level promoter structural variant; not directly assayable by the current SNP-focused search output.
functional_effects: Changes local promoter architecture and transcription-factor spacing in the 5' flanking region.
Usually interpreted as part of a promoter haplotype that can reshape regulatory context rather than coding sequence.
associated_conditions: Promoter-haplotype studies in schizophrenia and behavioral-genetics cohorts
research_context: This duplication is often discussed together with rs747302 and rs1800955 when papers examine combined promoter haplotypes.
Structural confirmation still requires a repeat- or structural-aware assay rather than a SNP-only VCF view.
variant_records.usual_variant_note: Often discussed alongside rs747302 and rs1800955 when reviewing DRD4 promoter haplotypes.
variant_records.methylation_interpretation: If the duplication is of interest, the methylation panel can still provide regional epigenetic context, but it does not substitute for a structural variant call.

evidence.label: PubMed 10329380: DRD4 promoter polymorphisms and transcriptional efficiency
PMC3151559: review of DRD4 polymorphisms and function
PMC4833125: promoter polymorphism reporter dataset

variant_records.clinical_interpretation: The exon III 48-bp VNTR is the most studied DRD4 gene-body variant, especially the 7-repeat allele, which has been associated at the research level with ADHD, novelty-seeking, addiction-related traits, and altered receptor signaling. Its effects are context-dependent and it is not a stand-alone diagnostic pathogenic variant.
variant_records.clinical_significance: Functional research variant; no definitive monogenic pathogenic assertion is seeded for this repeat.
functional_effects: Different repeat lengths alter the third intracellular loop of the receptor and are associated with differences in downstream dopamine signaling efficiency.
The 7-repeat allele is frequently described as a reduced-efficiency or altered-response receptor background compared with the common 4-repeat allele.
associated_conditions: Gene-by-environment studies of resilience and stress response
research_context: The 7R allele is a major focus in European-ancestry and American cohorts, while East Asian studies often consider 2R as a functionally analogous allele of interest.
This repeat requires repeat-aware genotyping and will usually not appear as a simple SNP hit in a standard VCF preview.
variant_records.usual_variant_note: The 7R allele is the canonical non-promoter DRD4 polymorphism discussed in behavioral-genetics literature.
variant_records.methylation_interpretation: The methylation panel can contextualize the gene-body environment around DRD4, but it does not directly genotype the exon III repeat. Dedicated repeat-aware assays are still required if the VNTR itself is the main target.

evidence.label: PMC3151559: DRD4 exon III VNTR review and functional context
PMC3653059: DRD4 exon III VNTR population and trait review
PMC3103527: functional grouping of DRD4 4R and 7R alleles
